user_prompt: Our privacy policy is 2 years old. Generate a checklist of what to update for current GDPR + ePrivacy.
actual_output:
## GDPR + ePrivacy Privacy Policy Update Checklist

*Compliance guidance only — not legal advice. Have counsel/DPO review before publication, especially if you operate across multiple EU/EEA Member States where ePrivacy cookie rules vary.*

---

# 1. Core GDPR Privacy Notice Requirements — Arts. 13–14

Your privacy policy should clearly disclose the following for **each processing activity**.

## Required identity and contact details

- [ ] Identify the **data controller** legal entity.
- [ ] Provide controller contact details.
- [ ] Provide EU/EEA representative details if required under **GDPR Art. 27**.
  - Required if you are outside the EU/EEA and offer goods/services to, or monitor behavior of, EU/EEA individuals, unless a narrow exemption applies.
- [ ] Provide Data Protection Officer contact details if appointed or mandatory under **Art. 37**.
- [ ] If no DPO, provide a privacy contact email or address.

---

# 2. Data Categories Collected

Update the policy to list all personal data currently collected, including:

- [ ] Account data: name, email, username, password hash, company, role.
- [ ] Billing data: address, VAT/tax ID, payment status, transaction metadata.
- [ ] Support data: tickets, chat logs, call recordings, attachments.
- [ ] Usage data: pages viewed, features used, clickstream events, session data.
- [ ] Device and technical data: IP address, browser, OS, device IDs, log files.
- [ ] Cookie and tracking data.
- [ ] Marketing data: newsletter preferences, campaign interactions, lead source.
- [ ] User-generated content.
- [ ] Communications data.
- [ ] Employee or applicant data, if the policy covers HR processing.
- [ ] Special category data, if any, under **Art. 9**.
- [ ] Children’s data, if any, under **Art. 8** and applicable national rules.

**Action:** Remove categories you no longer collect and add any new tools or data fields introduced in the last 2 years.

---

# 3. Purposes and Lawful Bases — Art. 6

For each purpose, list the applicable lawful basis. Do **not** assume consent is always appropriate.

| Processing Purpose | Likely Lawful Basis | GDPR Reference |
|---|---:|---|
| Creating and managing accounts | Contract performance | Art. 6(1)(b) |
| Providing the service | Contract performance | Art. 6(1)(b) |
| Customer support | Contract or legitimate interests | Art. 6(1)(b)/(f) |
| Security logs and fraud prevention | Legitimate interests/legal obligation | Art. 6(1)(f)/(c) |
| Billing and tax records | Legal obligation | Art. 6(1)(c) |
| Service improvement analytics | Legitimate interests or consent, depending on tracking | Art. 6(1)(f)/(a) |
| Email marketing to customers | Consent or soft opt-in, depending on local ePrivacy law | Art. 6(1)(a)/(f) + ePrivacy |
| Behavioral advertising/retargeting | Consent | Art. 6(1)(a) + ePrivacy |
| Cookies requiring consent | Consent | ePrivacy + Art. 6(1)(a), Art. 7 |
| Legal claims | Legitimate interests | Art. 6(1)(f) |
| Recruitment/HR processing | Contract/legal obligation/legitimate interests | Art. 6(1)(b)/(c)/(f) |

Checklist:

- [ ] Add a table mapping **each purpose** to:
  - data categories,
  - lawful basis,
  - retention period,
  - recipients/processors,
  - whether data is transferred internationally.
- [ ] For legitimate interests under **Art. 6(1)(f)**, disclose the specific legitimate interest.
- [ ] Confirm a Legitimate Interests Assessment has been completed where relying on **Art. 6(1)(f)**.
- [ ] For consent-based processing, explain that consent can be withdrawn at any time under **Art. 7(3)**.
- [ ] For special category data, identify both an **Art. 6** lawful basis and an **Art. 9** condition.

---

# 4. Cookie and Tracking Disclosures — ePrivacy + GDPR

A 2-year-old privacy/cookie policy is often outdated here.

## Cookie policy must disclose

- [ ] What cookies, SDKs, pixels, tags, local storage, mobile identifiers, and similar technologies you use.
- [ ] Cookie name or provider.
- [ ] Purpose.
- [ ] Duration/expiry.
- [ ] Category:
  - strictly necessary,
  - functional/preferences,
  - analytics,
  - advertising/marketing,
  - social media/embedded content.
- [ ] Whether data is shared with third parties.
- [ ] How users can manage or withdraw consent.

## Consent requirements

- [ ] Confirm non-essential cookies are not set until prior consent is obtained.
- [ ] Consent must be:
  - freely given,
  - specific,
  - informed,
  - unambiguous,
  - withdrawable,
  - documented.
- [ ] No pre-ticked boxes or implied consent through continued browsing.
- [ ] Provide equal ease for “accept” and “reject” where required by supervisory authority guidance.
- [ ] Offer granular choices by cookie category.
- [ ] Maintain consent logs to demonstrate compliance under **GDPR Art. 7(1)**.
- [ ] Provide a persistent “Cookie Settings” link to withdraw or modify consent.
- [ ] Review whether Google Analytics, Meta Pixel, LinkedIn Insight Tag, Hotjar, session replay, or similar tools require consent before activation.

## ePrivacy-specific items

- [ ] Check local Member State rules implementing the ePrivacy Directive.
- [ ] Confirm email/SMS marketing complies with opt-in or soft opt-in rules.
- [ ] Confirm direct marketing includes clear unsubscribe in every message.
- [ ] Review use of tracking pixels in marketing emails.
- [ ] Disclose any call recording, chat recording, or communications metadata collection where relevant.

---

# 5. International Data Transfers — Arts. 44–49

Update this if you use non-EEA vendors, cloud hosting, analytics, support, CRM, payment, AI, or marketing tools.

- [ ] List countries or categories of countries where data may be transferred.
- [ ] Identify transfer safeguards:
  - EU adequacy decision,
  - EU Standard Contractual Clauses,
  - UK IDTA/Addendum where applicable,
  - Binding Corporate Rules,
  - derogations under **Art. 49** only for exceptional cases.
- [ ] For US transfers, reference the **EU-U.S. Data Privacy Framework** where the vendor is certified.
- [ ] If relying on SCCs, confirm Transfer Impact Assessments are in place after **Schrems II**.
- [ ] Disclose how users can obtain a copy or summary of transfer safeguards.
- [ ] Check onward transfers by processors and sub-processors.

**High-risk update area:** If your old policy simply says “we may transfer data outside your country,” it is likely insufficient under **Arts. 13(1)(f), 14(1)(f), and 44–49**.

---

# 6. Processors, Vendors, and Sub-Processors — Art. 28

- [ ] Update the list of third-party processors used in the last 2 years:
  - hosting,
  - analytics,
  - CRM,
  - email delivery,
  - payment processors,
  - customer support tools,
  - error monitoring,
  - product analytics,
  - AI tools,
  - advertising platforms,
  - recruitment platforms.
- [ ] Confirm you have Data Processing Agreements with all processors under **Art. 28**.
- [ ] Confirm DPAs include:
  - subject matter and duration,
  - nature and purpose,
  - data categories,
  - data subject categories,
  - confidentiality,
  - security,
  - sub-processor controls,
  - assistance with DSARs,
  - breach notification support,
  - deletion/return at end of services,
  - audit rights.
- [ ] Link to a current sub-processor list if applicable.
- [ ] Describe categories of recipients in the privacy policy.
- [ ] Establish notice of new sub-processors if promised contractually.

---

# 7. Data Subject Rights — Arts. 12–23

Your policy should clearly explain EU/EEA rights:

- [ ] Right of access — **Art. 15**.
- [ ] Right to rectification — **Art. 16**.
- [ ] Right to erasure — **Art. 17**.
- [ ] Right to restriction — **Art. 18**.
- [ ] Right to data portability — **Art. 20**.
- [ ] Right to object — **Art. 21**.
- [ ] Right not to be subject to solely automated decisions with legal or similarly significant effects — **Art. 22**.
- [ ] Right to withdraw consent — **Art. 7(3)**.
- [ ] Right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority — **Art. 77**.

Operational updates:

- [ ] Add how users can submit a DSAR.
- [ ] State the standard response time: one month under **Art. 12(3)**.
- [ ] Explain possible extension by two further months for complex requests.
- [ ] Explain identity verification requirements.
- [ ] Provide contact details for rights requests.
- [ ] Confirm internal DSAR workflow exists and matches the policy.

---

# 8. Retention Periods — Art. 5(1)(e), Arts. 13–14

Generic wording like “we keep data as long as necessary” is usually insufficient on its own.

- [ ] Add specific retention periods or clear criteria for each category.
- [ ] Include retention for:
  - account data,
  - inactive accounts,
  - billing/tax records,
  - support tickets,
  - marketing contacts,
  - consent logs,
  - analytics data,
  - security logs,
  - backups,
  - recruitment data,
  - deleted user data,
  - legal hold exceptions.
- [ ] Explain deletion or anonymization procedures.
- [ ] Align policy wording with actual system retention settings.
- [ ] Document exceptions for legal obligations or legal claims.

Example:

| Data Category | Example Retention |
|---|---:|
| Account data | Life of account + 30–90 days |
| Billing/tax records | 6–10 years, depending on jurisdiction |
| Security logs | 90–365 days |
| Support tickets | 2–3 years |
| Marketing consent records | Duration of consent + limitation period |
| Backups | Rolling deletion within defined backup cycle |

---

# 9. Automated Decision-Making and Profiling — Art. 22

- [ ] State whether you conduct profiling or automated decision-making.
- [ ] If yes, explain:
  - logic involved,
  - significance and consequences,
  - whether decisions have legal or similarly significant effects,
  - user rights and human review options.
- [ ] Review personalization, risk scoring, fraud scoring, credit checks, hiring filters, AI recommendations, and dynamic pricing.
- [ ] If no qualifying automated decision-making occurs, say so clearly.

---

# 10. AI, Machine Learning, and Generative AI Processing

If added in the last 2 years, this likely needs new disclosure.

- [ ] Disclose use of AI tools that process personal data.
- [ ] Identify whether AI vendors are processors or independent controllers.
- [ ] Confirm DPAs are in place for AI processors under **Art. 28**.
- [ ] Confirm whether personal data is used to train models.
- [ ] If prompts, tickets, calls, or documents are sent to AI tools, disclose this.
- [ ] Add opt-out or control options where applicable.
- [ ] Assess whether AI processing involves profiling or automated decisions under **Art. 22**.
- [ ] Conduct a DPIA if AI creates high risk under **Art. 35**.

---

# 11. Security Measures — Art. 32

Privacy policies do not need to reveal sensitive technical details, but should describe safeguards at a high level.

- [ ] Update summary of security controls:
  - encryption in transit,
  - encryption at rest where applicable,
  - access controls,
  - MFA,
  - audit logging,
  - least privilege,
  - vulnerability management,
  - backups,
  - incident response,
  - vendor security review.
- [ ] Avoid absolute promises like “we guarantee security.”
- [ ] Confirm security claims are accurate and supported.

---

# 12. Data Breach Handling — Arts. 33–34

- [ ] Explain that you maintain procedures for personal data breaches.
- [ ] Ensure internal breach process supports notification to supervisory authority within 72 hours where required under **Art. 33**.
- [ ] Ensure process supports data subject notification where high risk under **Art. 34**.
- [ ] Avoid overpromising notification in all circumstances unless that is your policy.

---

# 13. Children’s Privacy — Art. 8

- [ ] State whether your service is intended for children.
- [ ] If relying on consent for information society services offered directly to children, verify Member State age thresholds.
- [ ] Add parental consent process if applicable.
- [ ] If not intended for children, state minimum age and deletion process if child data is discovered.

---

# 14. Marketing Communications — GDPR + ePrivacy

- [ ] Identify marketing data collected.
- [ ] Identify lawful basis for marketing processing.
- [ ] Distinguish between:
  - service emails,
  - transactional emails,
  - newsletter emails,
  - promotional emails,
  - B2B outreach,
  - SMS/phone marketing,
  - retargeting ads.
- [ ] Confirm opt-in/soft opt-in rules by country.
- [ ] Include unsubscribe instructions.
- [ ] Disclose use of marketing pixels and tracking.
- [ ] Maintain suppression lists for opt-outs.
- [ ] Avoid sending marketing based only on GDPR legitimate interests if ePrivacy requires consent.

---

# 15. Employee, Contractor, and Applicant Data

Often missed.

- [ ] Decide whether the public privacy policy covers HR data or whether you need a separate employee/applicant privacy notice.
- [ ] Include applicant tracking systems and recruitment vendors.
- [ ] Disclose background checks, reference checks, right-to-work checks, payroll, benefits, monitoring, device management, and productivity tools where applicable.
- [ ] Avoid relying on consent for employee processing unless genuinely freely given.
- [ ] Address local employment/privacy laws.

Relevant GDPR provisions include **Arts. 5, 6, 9, 13–14, 32, 88**.

---

# 16. Controller vs Processor Role Clarity

If you are a SaaS provider, you may act as both controller and processor.

- [ ] Explain when you act as controller, e.g.:
  - account management,
  - billing,
  - marketing,
  - website analytics,
  - security.
- [ ] Explain when you act as processor, e.g.:
  - customer-uploaded end-user data processed on behalf of business customers.
- [ ] Link to your DPA for customers where you are processor.
- [ ] Ensure privacy policy does not incorrectly claim controller status over all customer data.

Relevant provisions: **Arts. 4(7), 4(8), 13–14, 28**.

---

# 17. Sources of Personal Data — Art. 14

If you collect personal data indirectly, disclose sources.

- [ ] Lead enrichment providers.
- [ ] Public sources.
- [ ] Business partners.
- [ ] Customers uploading end-user data.
- [ ] Social media platforms.
- [ ] Data brokers.
- [ ] Referral sources.
- [ ] Recruitment agencies.

For indirectly collected data, include Art. 14 disclosures such as categories of data, source, and timing of notice.

---

# 18. Accuracy, Minimization, and Purpose Limitation — Art. 5

Check whether your policy and actual practices reflect GDPR principles:

- [ ] Collect only data needed for defined purposes — **Art. 5(1)(c)**.
- [ ] Use data only for disclosed compatible purposes — **Art. 5(1)(b)**.
- [ ] Keep data accurate and updated — **Art. 5(1)(d)**.
- [ ] Retain only as long as necessary — **Art. 5(1)(e)**.
- [ ] Secure data appropriately — **Art. 5(1)(f), Art. 32**.
- [ ] Maintain accountability documentation — **Art. 5(2)**.

---

# 19. Supervisory Authority and Complaint Rights — Art. 77

- [ ] Add the right to lodge a complaint with an EU/EEA supervisory authority.
- [ ] If you have an EU main establishment, identify the likely lead supervisory authority.
- [ ] If no EU establishment, provide practical guidance for users to contact their local authority.

---

# 20. Versioning and Change Notices

- [ ] Update effective date and last modified date.
- [ ] Maintain archived versions of previous policies.
- [ ] Explain how users will be notified of material changes.
- [ ] Ensure notice process matches contracts and product terms.
- [ ] Avoid retroactive expansion of data use without proper notice or consent where required.

---

# 21. High-Risk Processing and DPIAs — Art. 35

Check whether your policy should disclose or your internal documentation should assess:

- [ ] Large-scale systematic monitoring.
- [ ] Large-scale special category data.
- [ ] Behavioral profiling.
- [ ] Location tracking.
- [ ] Biometric or genetic data.
- [ ] AI-based scoring.
- [ ] Employee monitoring.
- [ ] Children’s data.
- [ ] Sensitive inferences.
- [ ] Combining datasets in unexpected ways.

If high-risk processing exists:

- [ ] Conduct or refresh a DPIA under **Art. 35**.
- [ ] Consult supervisory authority if residual high risk remains under **Art. 36**.
- [ ] Update privacy disclosures accordingly.

---

# 22. Practical Website/App Checks

Verify that the policy matches the real user experience.

- [ ] Privacy policy link visible at collection points.
- [ ] Cookie banner links to cookie policy/settings.
- [ ] Sign-up forms disclose key processing details.
- [ ] Newsletter forms include consent or soft opt-in wording.
- [ ] Contact forms include privacy notice link.
- [ ] In-app settings allow privacy choices where promised.
- [ ] DSAR email/contact form works.
- [ ] Unsubscribe links work.
- [ ] “Do not sell/share” or similar notices added if also subject to non-EU laws, e.g. CCPA/CPRA.
- [ ] Mobile app store privacy labels match actual SDKs and data practices.
- [ ] Consent management platform correctly blocks tags before consent.

---

# 23. Documents to Reconcile with the Privacy Policy

Update the privacy policy alongside these internal/external documents:

- [ ] Cookie policy.
- [ ] Terms of service.
- [ ] Data Processing Agreement.
- [ ] Sub-processor list.
- [ ] Records of Processing Activities — **Art. 30**.
- [ ] Lawful basis register.
- [ ] Legitimate Interests Assessments.
- [ ] DPIAs.
- [ ] Vendor/processor inventory.
- [ ] Data retention schedule.
- [ ] DSAR procedure.
- [ ] Breach response plan.
- [ ] Security policy.
- [ ] Marketing consent records.
- [ ] HR/applicant privacy notices.

---

# Priority Remediation Plan

## Week 1 — Discovery and inventory

- [ ] Inventory all personal data collected.
- [ ] Inventory all cookies, pixels, SDKs, and tags.
- [ ] Inventory all processors and sub-processors.
- [ ] Identify international transfers.
- [ ] Review all new tools added in the last 2 years, especially analytics, AI, CRM, support, marketing, and payment tools.

## Week 2 — Legal basis and disclosure mapping

- [ ] Map each processing purpose to lawful basis under **Art. 6**.
- [ ] Confirm any **Art. 9** special category data processing.
- [ ] Define retention periods.
- [ ] Confirm data subject rights workflow.
- [ ] Review marketing and cookie consent under ePrivacy.

## Week 3 — Draft updates

- [ ] Update privacy policy.
- [ ] Update cookie policy.
- [ ] Update consent banner wording and settings.
- [ ] Update processor/sub-processor disclosures.
- [ ] Update international transfer language.
- [ ] Update DSAR instructions.

## Week 4 — Implementation and evidence

- [ ] Publish updated policy.
- [ ] Archive previous version.
- [ ] Test cookie blocking before consent.
- [ ] Test DSAR intake.
- [ ] Verify unsubscribe and consent withdrawal.
- [ ] Store evidence of review and approvals.
- [ ] Schedule next policy review within 6–12 months.

---

# Highest-Risk Items to Check First

1. **Cookies/trackers firing before consent** — ePrivacy + GDPR Arts. 6, 7.  
2. **Missing lawful basis per processing purpose** — Art. 6.  
3. **Incomplete retention periods** — Art. 5(1)(e), Arts. 13–14.  
4. **Unlisted processors or no DPAs** — Art. 28.  
5. **Unaddressed international transfers** — Arts. 44–49.  
6. **No DSAR process/contact** — Arts. 12–23.  
7. **Use of AI tools without disclosure or DPA** — Arts. 5, 6, 13–14, 28, 35.  
8. **Marketing emails or pixels without valid ePrivacy compliance**.  
9. **Employee/applicant data ignored** — Arts. 13–14, 88.  
10. **Policy promises that do not match actual technical behavior** — Art. 5(2) accountability.
judge_reasoning: The chatbot provided a comprehensive and detailed checklist that directly addressed the user's request while adhering to its system prompt's instructions to cite GDPR articles and include a legal disclaimer.
